[13:42] a day or less.

[19:37] behind iron and ferritin.
[19:39] They are two different iron molecules.
[19:41] And so that's really important that we understand the differences.
[19:51] Um,
[19:55] it's not going to be much bigger on my screen.
[19:58] I'm going to be honest with you.
[19:59] And then I can't see half of it because of y'all.
[20:03] Um, so, um, keep in mind that iron is used, uh, for different purposes than ferritin.
[20:12] Ferritin is what we've always known as stored iron.
[20:16] Uh, we were basically taught not much else when we went through biology,
[20:22] patho, A and P, whatever in our, uh, prereqs and our RN programs.
[20:28] Um, but iron is for lots of body processes, your hormones, immune function,
[20:36] cellular growth, many, many, many, many processes use iron either as a basic
[20:42] ingredient or as some sort of catalyst or ion as processes occur.
[20:50] Um, and so it's really important that we get iron and ferritin in an optimal range.
[20:57] Um, ferritin, however, is used mostly for one main purpose, maybe a few other things,
[21:02] but the main purpose is that ferritin becomes hemoglobin on red blood cells.
[21:07] What do red blood cells do for us?
[21:10] What's their job?
[21:12] Why are red blood cells so important?
[21:18] Bury oxygen.
[21:19] They deliver oxygen to every single body cell from head to toe.
[21:24] What symptoms do you think are going to occur if that process is impaired?
[21:36] Fatigue, shortness of breath, tachycardia.
[21:40] Absolutely.
[21:41] Fatigue, uh, yeah, uh, tachycardia, palpitations.
[21:44] Absolutely.
[21:45] Because what is the backup for the red blood cells, your cardiac muscle?
[21:51] So if the cardiac muscle itself, the organ in your chest beating is getting some, uh,
[22:01] or getting a lack of oxygen delivery to that muscle, it just says, oh, well, I can
[22:06] just beat harder and faster.
[22:08] That'll deliver more oxygen, right?
[22:11] And that's an okay, temporary backup mechanism.
[22:14] But how long do you think that's going to work?
[22:18] It can't work a lifetime like that.
[22:21] Um, uh, you wind up with congestive heart failure and, um, hypertension.
[22:27] So, uh, not real good.
[22:30] And so again, we want to look at optimal functional, um, not necessarily, uh, typical
[22:36] ranges.
[22:38] So one of the first things I find when I see patients is patients come to me thinking
[22:45] they have hypothyroidism.
[22:47] And so they answer the thyroid questionnaire that I always send out.
[22:50] It's basically a simple review of systems.
[22:53] We're talking about their symptoms, but my TSH is always normal according to all
[22:58] the other labs they've had done.
[23:01] And so they, they think coming in that I'm just going to label them as hypothyroid
[23:06] and give them thyroid medicine, but the same exact symptoms happen with low iron
[23:10] and ferritin.
[23:11] So how can you distinguish between the two if you're not getting all of the lab
[23:16] values, so it's really important that we get the symptoms and we get, um, the
[23:24] labs to distinguish between the two.
[23:27] I have a boatload of patients that come to me saying they're losing their hair.
[23:30] Why?
[23:31] Well, these are some of the most common reasons.
[23:34] Well, if they're getting a low iron, low protein, low vitamin D, and they all
[23:39] have inflammation and insulin resistance, their hair is not going to grow well.
[23:43] And it's going to fall out.

[25:40] Medicare will cover almost all the labs that you want to get.

[25:50] justify your A1C screening for cardiovascular disease that will cover
[25:55] your CRP and sometimes, um, some of your other testing, uh, definitely a lipid
[26:01] panel, I don't draw too many lipids, but if you want to draw them, um,

[29:31] thing, lack of awareness or something for concentration.

[29:44] deficit disorder, but nobody's ever diagnosed it, I use, I throw that in as
[29:48] well, so I list every diagnosis code that matches symptoms to your patient.
[29:53] You can put 40 diagnosis codes on your lab order.
[29:57] It doesn't matter how many you use.

[33:01] Um, so when you look at, um, iron deficiencies, there's kind of stages
[33:08] So our goal is somewhere around 80 to a hundred, you can be 150, 160, um, you
[33:14] just don't want to be like screaming high.
[33:16] You don't want to be two or three, 400 for iron or ferritin.
[33:20] So number one, keep in mind that iron and ferritin have different measurements.
[33:28] Okay.
[33:30] Um, so we'll look at some of those in a little bit, but they're not the same
[33:35] measurement, they're not the same nanogram per meal, or micrograms per
[33:38] liter, um, uh, on every lab result.
[33:42] But our goal is to get the numbers pretty close together.
[33:47] Okay.
[33:49] Um, as iron deficiency develops though, a ferritin around 60 to 65 will
[33:55] start triggering some symptoms.
[33:57] People will start saying they have more fatigue, more brain fog.
[34:01] They can't get through the day very well in a year.
[34:03] They lose their energy.
[34:05] Um, they can't focus.
[34:07] Um, they'll start having some headaches randomly, um, maybe migraines that,
[34:13] and they've never had them before, um, as their iron and our ferritin specifically
[34:21] begins to fall further, those symptoms worsen.
[34:24] So now, instead of an occasional fatigue or an afternoon where I need a nap and
[34:29] can't get through the afternoon.
[34:31] Now it's every day.
[34:32] Like I have to stop and take a nap or I have to go get an energy drink.
[34:35] I get a, um, uh, cup of coffee, like whatever, an espresso, whatever, to
[34:43] keep me up and functioning the next few hours, um, sleep patterns begin to
[34:48] get disrupted because now they're using more caffeine, more stimulants to
[34:52] get through their day.
[34:53] So now they're awake all night and can't get to sleep at some point.
[34:58] Palpitations and tachycardia began, uh, dyspnea gets worse and worse.
[35:03] Other people are beginning to notice, are you okay?
[35:06] You're, you get winded when you go up those stairs, um, carrying the laundry.
[35:10] You, you seem short of breath.
[35:12] Are you all right?
[35:13] Um, and then as the ferritin level falls, they're, they get more and more depressed.
[35:18] It's not clinical depression.
[35:20] They just have no motivation.
[35:21] They have no energy.
[35:22] They literally just can't.
[35:24] And so, um, they call themselves, I feel like I'm depressed, but I'm really not.
[35:29] My brain tells me I'm not depressed.
[35:31] My body is telling me I am.
[35:33] That is what I talk about when I talk about poor motivation.
[35:37] If those, uh, ferritin levels continue to fall, um, they get down to 20 or less.
[35:43] You have pretty significant cardiac risk.
[35:45] That heart is really overworking and think about all these women, especially
[35:51] that have periods every month that are losing blood that are running in the
[35:54] twenties and thirties and then during their period week, that ferritin really
[35:58] plummets to like 10 or 15 and then they spend the next three or four weeks
[36:04] trying to recover from that.
[36:05] And then it happens again.
[36:06] So they're just going through this vicious cycle and they can't get their
[36:10] ferritin better, all these women on social media posts out there saying, I
[36:15] never get my ferritinites because they're never addressing it properly.
[36:18] We as providers have not done a very good job of helping patients understand
[36:24] what ferritin is doing and how the cells are responding to that lack.

[37:03] Yeah.
[37:03] So a ferritin of 13 is real terrible.
[37:06] Um, uh, amethyst, uh, what's the life cycle of the red blood cell?
[37:12] Do you remember?
[37:15] I'm not trying to put you on the spot.
[37:17] Uh, it's about a hundred days, 110 days.
[37:19] Yeah.

[37:24] that got infused gets put on, uh, new baby red blood cells.

[37:44] cell, that red blood cell has all the iron on it.
[37:47] It will ever have after it's in circulation.

[37:58] You can't do that.

[38:04] So you want to give them plenty of time, um, to, uh, get that fixed, uh, all even
[38:12] while they're getting infusions, you still want them taking some iron daily.

[38:47] Iron requires a few co-factors, vitamin C, of course.
[38:51] Um, I typically dose vitamin C, uh, two to 4,000 milligrams,
[38:56] three or four times a day.
[38:58] Yes.
[38:59] On purpose, not for iron absorption, but what else is going on?
[39:05] These people have terrible guts, terrible inflammation, lots of ROS, reactive
[39:11] oxidative species floating around in their bloodstream and they're making more.
[39:15] Um, and so vitamin C helps the body get rid of inflammation and focus
[39:20] on iron absorption improvements.
[39:23] Vitamin D is crucial.
[39:25] Um, some of you are aware that there are some small studies and some
[39:30] providers that are dosing iron every other day to minimize inflammation.
[39:36] Vitamin C will fix that problem when dosed enough.
[39:40] But the main reason they're dosing it every other day is because of hepcidin.
[39:45] Hepcidin is a hormone ish inflammation ish, um, a chemical that goes up
[39:53] when vitamin D is low.
[39:54] So if hepcidin is high, the body cannot absorb and utilize iron appropriately.
[40:00] And so the only way to lower hepcidin then is to increase vitamin D.
[40:05] And so that's why we use the osteopax because the vitamin D includes the
[40:09] boron, the vitamin K, the mag, all of the co-factors necessary.
[40:14] And so that really is helpful.
[40:16] Trace minerals like zinc and copper, manganese, molybdenum, um, uh, you
[40:24] know, all of those are in red meat.
[40:26] And so if they have iron deficiency anemia and their ferritin is low, the
[40:30] first food thing we're going to ask them to change is to eat more red meat.
[40:34] Therefore, they won't need a trace minerals peel if they are vegetarian
[40:38] or they don't eat or can't afford red meat, you may want to consider
[40:42] a trace minerals product, at least temporarily, but I never high dose
[40:47] trace minerals zinc, especially, um, is commonly being overdosed out there.

[40:58] getting any, or it's screaming high because they've been overdosing.

[41:07] it's helpful for my immune system.

[55:02] So never take them on an empty stomach, always in between bites of fatty food.

[55:41] several days a week, that's like hundreds of, um, uh, percent of the selenium you
[55:48] need, so like, uh, be careful telling people to do things, uh, when you're
[55:53] not sure what the nutrient content is.

[61:57] associated with the B12 deficiency.
[62:00] So, uh, just a few other supplement tips, um, uh, to think about Ferrosol
[62:11] is 45 milligrams per cap.
[62:12] They can only take one cap per dose.
[62:14] They can't double or triple that to get more without risking kidney injury.
[62:19] So be very careful with that.
[62:21] Um, lots of vitamin C, the more they take, the better off they are from head to toe.
[62:27] Um, there's lots of reasons to use high dose, use high doses of C.
[62:30] Uh, low dose C is sufficient for iron absorption, but it's not helpful for inflammation.
[62:36] It's not helpful for gut healing.
[62:38] It's not helpful for the constipation that often comes with Ferrosol.
[62:42] Um, even though Ferrosol is less troublesome to the gut, it is not no trouble.
[62:48] Like it's seal will cause some constipation or sluggish gut.
[62:52] So the C acts to counteract that.
[62:55] Once labs get to about 65 or 70, you might be able to take the patient off the iron peels,
[63:03] but they have to be consuming adequate red meat regularly, at least five out of seven days.
[63:10] If they are not, then you need to have some kind of plan so that they continue
[63:16] to get iron some days, but they get a break for it from it some days.
[63:21] Um, oftentimes I will have them take their iron Monday, Wednesday, Friday.
[63:25] Um, sometimes we'll take instead of BID, we'll just do QD.
[63:31] Um, but they absolutely cannot continue taking iron if they are not getting the lab values tested.
[63:38] So if something happens and they move or they lose insurance or whatever life brings their way and they're, you know, six months from now, they haven't gotten their labs.
[63:50] Um, they need to stop their iron.
[63:52] They need to know if they are not being followed for their iron lab work.
[63:58] They absolutely must stop taking their iron if they can't get labs done because it is toxic to the brain and to the liver.
[64:07] And so make sure they fully understand the risks to taking it, um, unchecked.
[64:13] I have a client with elevated ferritin.

[68:39] So be very careful putting patients on LIO.
[68:42] You can use centroid all day long, but not LIO.
[68:46] Lio-thyronine is T3.

[68:52] specific stimulant to the body.
[68:54] What did we already say is going to happen when ferritin is low and
[68:58] the heart kicks in as backup.
[69:01] We're going to have palpitations and tachycardia.
[69:03] We're going to run the risk of hypertension and CHF eventually.
[69:06] So because of the unsafe cardiac risks, we don't typically give low ferritin
[69:15] patients, Lio-thyronine, or if we do, we keep the dose really low, really tiny.
[69:20] Um, and so we'll talk next week about how we use the free T3 to guide
[69:24] your T3 dosing, and we use reverse T3 to guide your T4 or levodosing.

[73:50] amount of time they need them.

[73:55] will sustain those levels and minimize inflammation and insulin problems.
[74:00] If the level falls, a lot of times patients will get so excited.

[74:22] Well, that's why we fell.

[74:28] to how it was working.

[74:40] while and then learn to eat differently.

[74:46] Keep in mind your menstrual changes, um, in women, especially through
[74:50] menopause, perimenopause, really, really heavy bleeding will drive low ferritin.

[74:59] GYN people for an ablation or hysterectomy conversation.

[75:23] DNC that is like heat sealing your womb.

[75:29] You typically don't have bleeding, maybe spotting sometimes, but you
[75:33] don't have normal periods anymore.

[86:18] your ferritin is about one and a half times the iron.
[86:22] So in our case, we looked at the iron level was 87 or something,
[86:27] and the ferritin was 67.
[86:30] So that's not a big gap.

[86:54] it might not be outside range is too high.

[87:11] So if you have a ferritin level, that's a lot higher than your iron level,
[87:17] regard that as inflammation, not as iron.
